```{r}
  ) +
  tm_shape(am_pred_raster) +
  tm_raster(
    col.scale = tm_scale(values = "viridis", reverse = TRUE),
    col_alpha = 0.6,
    col.leg


```

To extract and compare predictor influence

```{r}
# Extract variable importance from each model
am_importance <- am_result$model@results
ax_importance <- ax_result$model@results

# Show only percent contributions
am_contrib <- am_importance[grep("contribution", rownames(am_importance)), , drop = FALSE]
ax_contrib <- ax_importance[grep("contribution", rownames(ax_importance)), , drop = FALSE]

# Show only permutation importance
am_perm <- am_importance[grep("permutation", rownames(am_importance)), , drop = FALSE]
ax_perm <- ax_importance[grep("permutation", rownames(ax_importance)), , drop = FALSE]

# Display nicely
print("Variable contribution - Acacia mearnsii:")
print(round(am_contrib, 2))

print("Variable contribution - Acacia melanoxylon:")
print(round(ax_contrib, 2))

print("Permutation importance - Acacia mearnsii:")
print(round(am_perm, 2))

print("Permutation importance - Acacia melanoxylon:")
print(round(ax_perm, 2))

```

Optional: Combine into data frames for comparison plots\

```{r}
# Convert to data frame for plotting
library(dplyr)

get_importance_df <- function(result, species_name) {
  imp <- result$model@results
  contrib <- imp[grep("contribution", rownames(imp)), , drop = FALSE]
  perm    <- imp[grep("permutation", rownames(imp)), , drop = FALSE]
  
  data.frame(
    variable = gsub(".contribution", "", rownames(contrib)),
    contribution = contrib[,1],
    permutation = perm[,1],
    species = species_name
  )
}

am_df <- get_importance_df(am_result, "A. mearnsii")
ax_df <- get_importance_df(ax_result, "A. melanoxylon")

# Combine
imp_df <- rbind(am_df, ax_df)

# Plot (optional - needs ggplot2)
library(ggplot2)
ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, -contribution), y = contribution, fill = species)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Predictor Contribution to Habitat Suitability",
       x = "Predictor", y = "Percent Contribution") +
  theme_minimal() +
  coord_flip()

```

```{r}
# Ensure your plot is saved as an object
library(ggplot2)

# Create the plot
p <- ggplot(imp_df, aes(x = reorder(variable, -contribution), y = contribution, fill = species)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  labs(title = "Predictor Contribution to Habitat Suitability",
       x = "Predictor", y = "Percent Contribution") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +  # Adjust base font size if needed
  coord_flip()

# Display the plot
print(p)

# Export to high-resolution PNG
ggsave("predictor_contribution.png", plot = p, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 600)

# Export to vector SVG format
ggsave("predictor_contribution.svg", plot = p, width = 10, height = 6, dpi = 600)

```

Add Model Validation (AUC)

### Why it matters:

AUC (Area Under the Curve) helps evaluate how well the MaxEnt model
discriminates presence from background points. AUC \> 0.7 is acceptable;
\>0.9 is excellent.

```{r}
# View evaluation metrics
am_eval <- am_result$model@results
ax_eval <- ax_result$model@results

# Print AUC scores
cat("AUC - Acacia mearnsii:", round(am_eval["Training.AUC", ], 3), "\n")
cat("AUC - Acacia melanoxylon:", round(ax_eval["Training.AUC", ], 3), "\n")

```

Plot Variable Response Curves

Response curves help interpret the **ecological behavior** of each
species — e.g., is suitability increasing with rainfall, or is there a
threshold?

```{r}
# Plot variable response curves for Acacia mearnsii
response(am_result$model)

# Plot variable response curves for Acacia melanoxylon
response(ax_result$model)

```

Visual Comparison of Key Predictors\
Visualizing predictors like soil or rainfall across space can help guide
**management strategies** — e.g., focus removal efforts where high
suitability aligns with these factors.\

```{r}
# Plot key predictor maps
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plot(bio5_crop_rs, main = "Temp W.Month (bio5)") #Temperature of Warmest Month
plot(soil_crop_rs, main = "Soil Type")
plot(landcover_crop_rs, main = "land cover")
```

```{r}
# Plot key predictor maps
par(mfrow = c(1, 3))
plot(slope_crop_rs, main = "Slope")
plot(bio18_crop_rs, main = "Precipitation of Warmest Quarter")
plot(bio14_crop_rs, main = "Precipitation of Driest Month ")
```

## **Use Variable Importance to Guide Management**

-   If **soil** drives *A. mearnsii*, map soil types to **prioritize
    control** zones.

    If **precipitation and slope** drive *A. melanoxylon*, prioritize
    **steep, wet areas**.

    ```{r}
    # Example: Binary habitat suitability
    am_binary <- am_result$prediction > 0.5
    ax_binary <- ax_result$prediction > 0.5

    # Overlay with soil or slope
    plot(am_binary, main = "A. mearnsii Suitable Areas")
    plot(soil_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
    #plot(bio5_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
    #plot(landcover_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
    ```

```{r}
# Example: Binary habitat suitability
ax_binary <- ax_result$prediction > 0.5

# Overlay with soil or slope
plot(ax_binary , main = "A. melanoxylon Suitable Areas")
#plot(slope_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
plot(bio18_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
#plot(bio14_crop_rs, add = TRUE, alpha = 0.4)
```

```{r}
library(terra)

# Recreate raster_list without ndvi
raster_list <- list(
  bio1_cu_rs, bio4_cu_rs, bio5_cu_rs, bio6_cu_rs, bio8_cu_rs,
  bio10_cu_rs, bio12_cu_rs, bio14_cu_rs, bio15_cu_rs, bio18_cu_rs,
  dem_rs, slope_rs, landcover_rs, soil_rs_f,
   bulky_density_rs, clay_content_rs,
  coarse_fragment_rs, sand_content_rs, silt_content_rs,
  nitrogen_content_rs, organic_matter_rs, distance_nearest_rs
)

# Assign clean names (ndvi removed)
names(raster_list) <- c(
  "bio1", "bio4", "bio5", "bio6", "bio8",
  "bio10", "bio12", "bio14", "bio15", "bio18",
  "dem", "slope", "landcover", "soil",
   "bulky_density", "clay_content",
  "coarse_fragment", "sand_content", "silt_content",
  "nitrogen_content", "organic_matter", "distance_nearest"
)

# Append ndvi_longterm
raster_list[["ndvi_longterm"]] <- ndvi_longterm_rs
raster_list[["moisture_longterm"]] <- moisture_longterm_rs
# Check final length
length(raster_list)
# Should return 24

# Quick check of names and classes
lapply(raster_list, check_rasters)


```

```{r}
library(terra)

# Function to safely get number of layers
safe_nlyr <- function(x) {
  tryCatch(nlyr(x), error = function(e) NA)
}

# Check each raster
nlyr_values <- sapply(raster_list, safe_nlyr)
nlyr_values
# NA indicates a broken raster

# Remove broken rasters
raster_list <- raster_list[!is.na(nlyr_values)]

# Now sapply(raster_list, nlyr) should work
sapply(raster_list, nlyr)

```

```{r}
  bio1_cu_rs, bio4_cu_rs, bio5_cu_rs, bio6_cu_rs, bio8_cu_rs,
  bio10_cu_rs, bio12_cu_rs, bio14_cu_rs, bio15_cu_rs, bio18_cu_rs,

  coarse_fragment_rs, sand_content_rs, silt_content_rs,
```

PREPARATION OF PREDICTORS

```{r}
# === STACK CURRENT PREDICTORS ===
# Load terra explicitly to ensure you're using its functions
library(terra)

# Combine SpatRasters using terra::c()
stack_current <- c(
  bio1_cu_rs, bio4_cu_rs, bio5_cu_rs, bio6_cu_rs, bio8_cu_rs,
  bio10_cu_rs, bio12_cu_rs, bio14_cu_rs, bio15_cu_rs, bio18_cu_rs,
  dem_rs, slope_rs, landcover_rs, soil_rs_f,
   moisture_longterm_rs, bulky_density_rs, clay_content_rs,
  coarse_fragment_rs, sand_content_rs, silt_content_rs,
  nitrogen_content_rs, organic_matter_rs, distance_nearest_rs,ndvi_longterm_rs
)

# Rename layers
# Assign correct names
names(stack_current) <- c(
  paste0("bio", c(1,4,5,6,8,10,12,14,15,18), "_cur"),
  "dem", "slope", "landcoverchange", "soil",
  names(moisture_longterm_rs), "bulky_density", "clay_content",
  "coarse_fragment", "sand_content", "silt_content",
  "nitrogen_content", "organic_matter", "distance_nearest",
  names(ndvi_longterm_rs) # automatically adds: ndvi_mean, ndvi_sd, ndvi_min, ndvi_max
)


```

```{r}
# Check which layers in the stack are fully NA
na_check <- sapply(1:nlyr(stack_current), function(i) {
  layer_name <- names(stack_current)[i]
  layer_vals <- values(stack_current[[i]])
  is_empty <- all(is.na(layer_vals))
  cat(layer_name, " -- All NA:", is_empty, "\n")
  return(is_empty)
})
```

```{r}
})
print(na_proportion)
```

```{r}
plot(valid_mask, main = "Common Valid Pixels")

```

```{r}
# Crop stack to common valid region

# Optional: trim to remove surrounding NA borders
stack_valid_trimmed <- trim(stack_valid)
```

```{r}
plot(stack_valid_trimmed)       # Small multiple view (if not too many layers)

```

```{r}
#library(terra)

# Stack your environmental variables into one RasterStack (assuming they are already cropped/masked)
#stack_current <- rast(list(dem_crop, slope_crop, soil_crop, bio1_crop, bio12_crop, bio4_crop, bio5_crop, bio8_crop, bio14_crop, bio15_crop, bio18_crop, bio6_crop, bio10_crop))

# Create a mask raster: 1 where all layers are non-NA, NA elsewhere
valid_mask <- app(stack_current, fun = function(...) all(!is.na(c(...)))) 

# Mask valid_mask by the polygon to ensure it only covers your study areas
valid_mask <- mask(valid_mask, gmb_reserve_vect)





```

```{r}
# Save valid_mask as bias file for Maxent
# Write ASCII raster as bias file
writeRaster(valid_mask, "bias_mask.asc", overwrite=TRUE, filetype="AAIGrid", datatype="INT1U")
```

```{r}
# Test reading
bias_test <- rast("bias_mask.asc")
plot(bias_test)

```

-   Your environmental rasters can be clipped and masked to this polygon
    to ensure only relevant areas are included.

    However, since your study areas are spatially separate (likely two
    polygons merged), background points could still be sampled from
    areas connecting or near them but not studied, which could bias
    Maxent.\
    SEPARATE AREAS\
    DUE TO Gishwati and Mukura have very different ecological
    characteristics If you find these areas are ecologically distinct
    (e.g., different vegetation types, climate, species composition),
    consider running separate Maxent models for each polygon (Option 2).
    You can identify polygons with clumps() or simply split your
    polygons and repeat the process for each. This avoids mixing data
    from disconnected ecological spaces.

-Separate your study area polygons\

```{r}
library(terra)

# Convert SpatVector to sf to use dplyr easily (optional)
library(sf)
gmb_reserve_sf <- sf::st_as_sf(gmb_reserve_vect)

# Split by source area (assuming source field groups polygons by site)
gishwati_sf <- subset(gmb_reserve_sf, grepl("Gishwati", source))
mukura_sf <- subset(gmb_reserve_sf, grepl("Mukura", source))

# Dissolve polygons for each site into one polygon each (union)
gishwati_union <- sf::st_union(gishwati_sf)
mukura_union <- sf::st_union(mukura_sf)



```

```{r}
library(sf)

# Fix only the invalid feature
mukura_sf[1, ] <- st_make_valid(mukura_sf[1, ])

# Double-check validity
st_is_valid(mukura_sf)
```

```{r}
gishwati_union <- st_union(gishwati_sf)
mukura_union   <- st_union(mukura_sf)

# Convert back to SpatVector for terra
gishwati_vect <- vect(gishwati_union)
mukura_vect <- vect(mukura_union)

```

\
#above is good

```{r}
#skip this as above code works
# Check attributes of the SpatVector
head(gmb_reserve_vect)
library(terra)



# Subset directly in terra by "source" field
gishwati_vect <- subset(gmb_reserve_vect, gmb_reserve_vect$source %in% c("Gishwati_core", "Gishwati_buffer"))
mukura_vect   <- subset(gmb_reserve_vect, gmb_reserve_vect$source %in% c("Mukura_core", "Mukura_buffer"))

# Dissolve (aggregate) polygons for each site
gishwati_union <- aggregate(gishwati_vect)
mukura_union   <- aggregate(mukura_vect)

```

```{r}
library(terra)


# Subset directly in terra by "source" field
gishwati_vect <- subset(gmb_reserve_vect, gmb_reserve_vect$source %in% c("Gishwati_core", "Gishwati_buffer"))
mukura_vect   <- subset(gmb_reserve_vect, gmb_reserve_vect$source %in% c("Mukura_core", "Mukura_buffer"))

# Dissolve (aggregate) polygons for each site
gishwati_union <- aggregate(gishwati_vect)
mukura_union   <- aggregate(mukura_vect)

```

\###\
Export NDVI Mean

Gishwati NDVI

project **vector → raster**

```{r}
gishwati_vect <- project(gishwati_vect, ndvi_mean)
mukura_vect   <- project(mukura_vect, ndvi_mean)

```

```{r}
ndvi_gishwati <- ndvi_mean |>
  crop(gishwati_vect) |>
  mask(gishwati_vect)
ndvi_mukura <- ndvi_mean |>
  crop(mukura_vect) |>
  mask(mukura_vect)


```

```{r}
green_palette <- colorRampPalette(c(
  "#e5f5e0",
  "#a1d99b",
  "#31a354",
  "#006d2c"
))(100)

```

```{r}
  axes = TRUE
)
plot(gishwati_vect, add = TRUE, lwd = 2)

```

```{r}
  tm_layout(
    main.title = "NDVI Mean — Gishwati",
    legend.outside = TRUE
  )
```

```{r}
ndvi_stacki <- terra::rast(ndvi_stack)
```

\###

## Crop, mask, and prepare environmental variables separately

```{r}
crop_mask_env <- function(rast_list, polygon_vect) {
  cropped_list <- lapply(rast_list, function(r) {
    cropped <- crop(r, polygon_vect)
    masked <- mask(cropped, polygon_vect)
    return(masked)
  })
  return(cropped_list)
}

```

Apply to both areas

```{r}
library(terra)

# Reproject vectors to match the raster CRS
gishwati_vect_utm <- project(gishwati_vect, crs(stack_current))
mukura_vect_utm  <- project(mukura_vect,  crs(stack_current))


```

```{r}
# Your list of environmental rasters
#stack_current <- list(dem_rwanda2, slope, soil2006, bio1, bio12, bio4, bio5, bio8, bio14, bio15, bio18, bio6, bio10......)

# For Gishwati
gishwati_env <- crop_mask_env(stack_current, gishwati_vect_utm)

# For Mukura
mukura_env <- crop_mask_env(stack_current, mukura_vect_utm)

```

```{r}
crs(stack_current)
crs(gishwati_vect)
crs(mukura_vect)
```

```{r}
# Load necessary libraries
library(terra)

# Step 1: Convert to `SpatVector` with correct CRS (WGS84, degrees)
presence_vect <- vect(presence_points, geom = c("Central_coordinate_Longitude", "Central_coordinate_Latitude"), crs = "EPSG:4326")

# Step 2: Reproject points to match raster CRS
presence_vect_proj <- project(presence_vect, crs(stack_valid_trimmed))

# OPTIONAL: Repeat for background points if needed
background_vect <- vect(background_points, geom = c("Central_coordinate_Longitude", "Central_coordinate_Latitude"), crs = "EPSG:4326")
background_vect_proj <- project(background_vect, crs(stack_valid_trimmed))

```

```{r}
# Project polygons into the same CRS as the presence points
gishwati_vect_proj <- project(gishwati_vect, crs(presence_vect_proj))
mukura_vect_proj   <- project(mukura_vect,   crs(presence_vect_proj))

```

```{r}
presence_gishwati   <- presence_vect_proj[gishwati_vect_proj, ]
background_gishwati <- background_vect_proj[gishwati_vect_proj, ]

presence_mukura   <- presence_vect_proj[mukura_vect_proj, ]
background_mukura <- background_vect_proj[mukura_vect_proj, ]

```

skip:Subset presence and background points by polygon

```{r}
presence_gishwati <- presence_vect_proj[gishwati_vect, ]
background_gishwati <- background_vect_proj[gishwati_vect, ]

presence_mukura <- presence_vect_proj[mukura_vect, ]
background_mukura <- background_vect_proj[mukura_vect, ]

```

Run Maxent models separately

**Explore variable importance separately first**

Run preliminary Maxent or other models **separately for Gishwati and
Mukura** using your current presence and background points per area.

Extract variable importance, jackknife tests, response curves. Compare
results to identify consistent vs. unique important variables.\
\

```{r}
library(terra)
library(raster)

# 1. Combine list of SpatRaster → one SpatRaster
gishwati_spat <- rast(gishwati_env)   # combines the list
mukura_spat   <- rast(mukura_env)

# 2. Convert SpatRaster → RasterStack
gishwati_stack <- stack(gishwati_spat)
mukura_stack   <- stack(mukura_spat)


```

```{r}
# Convert to matrix/dataframe of xy
presence_gishwati_xy   <- crds(presence_gishwati, df=TRUE)
background_gishwati_xy <- crds(background_gishwati, df=TRUE)

presence_mukura_xy   <- crds(presence_mukura, df=TRUE)
background_mukura_xy <- crds(background_mukura, df=TRUE)

```

```{r}
plot(presence_gishwati_xy)
```

```{r}
nrow(background_gishwati )
```

\
Running this model code, it returns na errors

```{r}
library(dismo)

model_gishwati <- maxent(
  x = gishwati_stack,
  p = presence_gishwati_xy,
  a = background_gishwati_xy,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

model_mukura <- maxent(
  x = mukura_stack,
  p = presence_mukura_xy,
  a = background_mukura_xy,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

```

Model Gishwati is indicating 2 of the presence points have NA predictor
values

Let check those points

1\. Extract predictor values at presence points :This gives you a
**matrix/data.frame** of predictor values at each presence point.

```{r}
# Extract predictor values for each presence point
pres_vals <- raster::extract(gishwati_stack, presence_gishwati_xy)

```

2\. Find which rows have NA values:This shows you the **coordinates of
the points with NA values**.

```{r}
# Identify which rows have NA
na_rows <- which(!complete.cases(pres_vals))

# Print the problematic points
presence_gishwati_xy[na_rows, ]

```

### Optional: bind coordinates + predictor values

So you can see exactly *which variable(s)* is NA for each point:

```{r}
problem_points <- cbind(presence_gishwati_xy[na_rows, ], pres_vals[na_rows, ])
problem_points

```

Check predictor coverage

```{r}

```

```{r}
plot(gishwati_stack[["distance_nearest"]], main="distance_nearest")
points(presence_gishwati_xy, pch=20, col="blue")
points(presence_gishwati_xy[na_rows, ], pch=20, col="red")

```

```{r}

# Extract predictor values for each presence point
pres_vals2 <- raster::extract(mukura_stack, presence_mukura_xy)


```

```{r}
# Identify which rows have NA
na_rows <- which(!complete.cases(pres_vals2))

# Print the problematic points
presence_mukura_xy[na_rows, ]
```

```{r}
problem_points2 <- cbind(presence_mukura_xy[na_rows, ], pres_vals2[na_rows, ])
problem_points2
```

```{r}
plot(mukura_stack[["soil"]], main="soil")
points(presence_mukura_xy, pch=20, col="blue")
points(presence_mukura_xy[na_rows, ], pch=20, col="red")
```

After observing that points that have NA predictors are close to those
have one we decide to use :We’ll use **nearest neighbor filling**
(`terra::focal` + `na.fill`) so NA cells in your predictor rasters get
values from the nearest valid neighbor. That way, when you extract
values at presence points, they won’t be NA anymore.

```{r}
library(terra)


# Extract the raster layer
dist_ras <- gishwati_stack[["distance_nearest"]]

# Define a 3x3 neighborhood
w <- matrix(1, nrow = 3, ncol = 3)

# Fill NAs using mean of neighbors
dist_ras_filled <- focal(dist_ras, w = w, fun = mean, na.policy = "only", na.rm = TRUE)

# Replace the original layer in the stack (optional)
gishwati_stack[["distance_nearest"]] <- dist_ras_filled


# Example: fix soil predictor in Mukura
soil_ras <- mukura_stack[["soil"]]

# Fill NAs using nearest neighbor
soil_ras_filled <- terra::focal(soil_ras, w = w, fun = mean, na.policy = "only", na.rm = TRUE)


# Replace in stack
mukura_stack[["soil"]] <- soil_ras_filled

```

## \*\* \*\*Check after filling

```{r}
# Re-extract predictor values at presence points
pres_vals_gishwati <- terra::extract(gishwati_stack, presence_gishwati_xy)
sum(!complete.cases(pres_vals_gishwati))  # should be 0

pres_vals_mukura <- terra::extract(mukura_stack, presence_mukura_xy)
sum(!complete.cases(pres_vals_mukura))  # should be 0

```

## **Mukura predictors is fixed but Gishwati stlls have two NA.** Try a larger neighborhood. Instead of `3×3`, **3×3** looks at the target cell + its immediate 8 neighbors. expand to `5×5` or `7×7`. looks at the target cell + 48 surrounding cells.7 x7 return 0

```{r}
# Define a bigger neighborhood (5x5 window)
w5 <- matrix(1, nrow = 7, ncol = 7)

dist_ras_filled <- focal(dist_ras, w = w5, fun = mean, na.policy = "only", na.rm = TRUE)
gishwati_stack[["distance_nearest"]] <- cover(dist_ras, dist_ras_filled)

# Re-check
pres_vals_gishwati <- terra::extract(gishwati_stack, presence_gishwati_xy)
sum(!complete.cases(pres_vals_gishwati))

```

## **REDO THE MODEL AFTER FIXING THE PREDICTORS NA** Convert `terra` objects to what `dismo` expects

first Check your inputs before `maxent()`

```{r}
#str(gishwati_stack_r)   # should be a RasterStack
head(presence_gishwati_xy)
head(background_gishwati_xy)
nrow(presence_gishwati_xy)
nrow(background_gishwati_xy)

```

Background information from each site Gishwati vs Mukura

```{r}
library(dismo)
# Background points for Gishwati: exclude presences
background_gishwati_xy <- randomPoints(
  mask = gishwati_stack_r,          # predictor raster
  n = 1000,                         # desired number
  p = presence_gishwati_xy,         # exclude presence locations
  excludep = TRUE,                  # enforce exclusion
  ext = NULL,                       # full raster extent or your polygon
  extf = 1.0
)

```

```{r}
background_mukura_xy <- randomPoints(
  mask = mukura_stack_r,
  n = 1000,
  p = presence_mukura_xy,
  excludep = TRUE
)

```

```{r}
library(terra)
library(raster)
library(dismo)

# Convert terra stack -> raster stack
gishwati_stack_r <- stack(gishwati_stack)
mukura_stack_r   <- stack(mukura_stack)

# Ensure presence/background are matrices or data.frames
presence_gishwati_xy <- as.data.frame(presence_gishwati_xy, xy=TRUE)[, c("x", "y")]
background_gishwati_xy <- as.data.frame(background_gishwati_xy, xy=TRUE)[, c("x", "y")]

presence_mukura_xy <- as.data.frame(presence_mukura_xy, xy=TRUE)[, c("x", "y")]
background_mukura_xy <- as.data.frame(background_mukura_xy, xy=TRUE)[, c("x", "y")]


```

#Skip this step as there na in background

```{r}
# Run Maxent again
model_gishwati <- maxent(
  x = gishwati_stack_r,
  p = presence_gishwati_xy,
  a = background_gishwati_xy,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

model_mukura <- maxent(
  x = mukura_stack_r,
  p = presence_mukura_xy,
  a = background_mukura_xy,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

```

```{r}
# Extract predictor values at presences again (from raster stack)
pres_vals_gishwati <- raster::extract(gishwati_stack_r, presence_gishwati_xy)

# Find rows with NA
na_rows_gishwati <- which(!complete.cases(pres_vals_gishwati))

# Inspect them
cbind(presence_gishwati_xy[na_rows_gishwati, ], pres_vals_gishwati[na_rows_gishwati, ])

```

```{r}
# Extract predictor values at background points
back_vals_gishwati <- raster::extract(gishwati_stack_r, background_gishwati_xy)

# Find rows with NA
na_rows_back <- which(!complete.cases(back_vals_gishwati))

# Inspect them
cbind(background_gishwati_xy[na_rows_back, ], back_vals_gishwati[na_rows_back, ])

```

```{r}
# Extract predictor values at presences again (from raster stack)
pres_vals_mukura <- raster::extract(mukura_stack_r, presence_mukura_xy)

# Find rows with NA
na_rows_mukura <- which(!complete.cases(pres_vals_mukura))

# Inspect them
cbind(presence_mukura_xy[na_rows_mukura, ], pres_vals_mukura[na_rows_mukura, ])

```

```{r}
# Extract predictor values at background points
back_vals_mukura <- raster::extract(mukura_stack_r, background_mukura_xy)

# Find rows with NA
na_rows_back <- which(!complete.cases(back_vals_mukura))

# Inspect them
cbind(background_mukura_xy[na_rows_back, ], back_vals_mukura[na_rows_back, ])

```

## Since the **NA predictors are in the background points**, the cleanest fix is to **filter them out before running Maxent**.

```{r}
# --- Gishwati ---
# Extract predictor values for background
back_vals_gishwati <- raster::extract(gishwati_stack_r, background_gishwati_xy)

# Keep only rows without NA
valid_idx_gishwati <- complete.cases(back_vals_gishwati)
background_gishwati_xy_clean <- background_gishwati_xy[valid_idx_gishwati, ]

cat("Removed", sum(!valid_idx_gishwati), "NA background points from Gishwati\n")

# --- Mukura ---
back_vals_mukura <- raster::extract(mukura_stack_r, background_mukura_xy)

valid_idx_mukura <- complete.cases(back_vals_mukura)
background_mukura_xy_clean <- background_mukura_xy[valid_idx_mukura, ]

cat("Removed", sum(!valid_idx_mukura), "NA background points from Mukura\n")

```

```{r}

model_gishwati <- maxent(
  x = gishwati_stack_r,
  p = presence_gishwati_xy,
  a = background_gishwati_xy_clean,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

model_mukura <- maxent(
  x = mukura_stack_r,
  p = presence_mukura_xy,
  a = background_mukura_xy_clean,
  args = c("jackknife=true", "responsecurves=true", "replicates=1", "randomseed=true")
)

```

```{r}
library(terra)

# Define output directory
output_dir <- "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Gishwati_Mukura"

# ---- Export Raster Stacks ----

# Export Gishwati raster stack
writeRaster(
  gishwati_stack_r,
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "Gishwati_Stack_All_Variables.tif"),
  overwrite = TRUE
)

# Export Mukura raster stack
writeRaster(
  mukura_stack_r,
  filename = file.path(output_dir, "Mukura_Stack_All_Variables.tif"),
  overwrite = TRUE
)

message("Raster stacks exported successfully.")

# ---- Export Presence and Background Points ----

# Gishwati presence
write.csv(
  presence_gishwati_xy,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "Presence_Gishwati_Clean.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

# Gishwati background
write.csv(
  background_gishwati_xy_clean,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "Background_Gishwati_Clean.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

# Mukura presence
write.csv(
  presence_mukura_xy,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "Presence_Mukura_Clean.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

# Mukura background
write.csv(
  background_mukura_xy_clean,
  file = file.path(output_dir, "Background_Mukura_Clean.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

message("Presence and background CSV files exported successfully.")

```

```{r}
background_gishwati_xy_clean<- read.csv(file.path(shapefile_dir, "Background_Gishwati_Clean.csv"))
background_mukura_xy_clean<-read.csv(file.path(shapefile_dir, "Background_Mukura_Clean.csv"))
  
```

## \*\* Step 1: Extract variable importance\*\*

Maxent models in `dismo` have a `results` slot where you can extract
variable importance (% contribution, permutation importance, etc.).

```{r}
# Extract variable importance tables
imp_gishwati <- model_gishwati@results
imp_mukura   <- model_mukura@results

# Keep only variable importance rows
vars_gishwati <- imp_gishwati[grep("contribution", rownames(imp_gishwati)), , drop=FALSE]
vars_mukura   <- imp_mukura[grep("contribution", rownames(imp_mukura)), , drop=FALSE]

# Convert to data.frame for comparison
vars_gishwati_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(vars_gishwati),
  Contribution = vars_gishwati[,1],
  row.names = NULL
)

vars_mukura_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(vars_mukura),
  Contribution = vars_mukura[,1],
  row.names = NULL
)

```

## **Step 2: Extract permutation importance**

Permutation importance is another key indicator from Maxent:

```{r}
# Extract permutation importance
perm_gishwati <- imp_gishwati[grep("permutation.importance", rownames(imp_gishwati)), , drop=FALSE]
perm_mukura   <- imp_mukura[grep("permutation.importance", rownames(imp_mukura)), , drop=FALSE]

perm_gishwati_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(perm_gishwati),
  PermutationImportance = perm_gishwati[,1],
  row.names = NULL
)

perm_mukura_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = rownames(perm_mukura),
  PermutationImportance = perm_mukura[,1],
  row.names = NULL
)

```

## **Step 3: Extract jackknife results**

Jackknife test results can be plotted directly:

```{r}

# Jackknife variable importance plot
plot(model_gishwati)

```

Export Figures\

```{r}
# Save Jackknife plot in PDF with publication quality

# Export Jackknife plot with readable axis + symbols
pdf(
  file = "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/jackknife_gishwati.pdf",
  width = 80/25.4,   # 80 mm wide
  height = 80/25.4  # slightly taller for readability
)

# Adjust margins so labels don’t get cut off
par(mar = c(5, 5, 4, 2))  

# Plot jackknife with enhanced readability
plot(model_gishwati, type = "jackknife",
     main = "Jackknife Test - Gishwati",
     cex.axis = 0.8,   # axis numbers bigger
     cex.lab = 1.0,    # axis titles bigger
     cex.main = 1,   # main title bigger
     cex = 0.5,        # symbol/bar size inside plot
     lwd = 2)          # line/bar thickness

dev.off()


```

```{r}
plot(model_mukura)
```

```{r}
# Export Jackknife plot for Mukura with readable axis + symbols
pdf(
  file = "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/jackknife_mukura.pdf",
  width = 80/25.4,   # 80 mm wide
  height = 80/25.4   # 80 mm tall
)

# Adjust margins (bottom, left, top, right)
par(mar = c(5, 5, 4, 2))  

# Plot jackknife with enhanced readability
plot(model_mukura, type = "jackknife",
     main = "Jackknife Test - Mukura",
     cex.axis = 0.8,   # axis numbers larger
     cex.lab = 1.0,    # axis labels larger
     cex.main = 1,   # main title larger
     cex = 0.5,        # symbol/bar size inside plot
     lwd = 2)          # line/bar thickness

dev.off()



```

NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) ranges theoretically from
**-1 to +1**:

-   **-1 to 0** → Typically water, snow, clouds, or non-vegetated
    surfaces.

    **0 to 0.2** → Bare soil, rocks, sand, or built-up areas with little
    to no vegetation.

    **0.2 to 0.5** → Sparse vegetation (shrubs, grasslands, dry
    croplands).

    **0.5 to 0.8+** → Dense, healthy vegetation (forests, well-irrigated
    crops).

    So in your case:

👉 **NDVI between -1 and +0.2** suggests **non-vegetated areas**:

Values closer to **-1** → water, clouds, or snow.

-   Around **0** → bare ground, rocks, or urban/built-up areas.

    Up to **0.2** → possibly dry soil or sparse, stressed vegetation.

```{r}
plot(mukura_stack_r[["sfsm_mean"]], main="moisture")
points(presence_mukura_xy, pch=20, col="blue")

```

```{r}
plot(gishwati_stack_r[["bulky_density"]], main="bulky")
points(presence_gishwati_xy, pch=20, col="blue")

```

## \*\* Step 4: Extract response curves\*\*

Maxent will generate response curves (if `responsecurves=true` was set):

```{r}
resp_gishwati <- response(model_gishwati)
resp_mukura   <- response(model_mukura)

```

## **Step 5: Compare results**

Now you can compare contributions between Gishwati and Mukura:

```{r}
library(dplyr)

# Merge contribution tables
compare_vars <- full_join(
  vars_gishwati_df %>% rename(Gishwati = Contribution),
  vars_mukura_df %>% rename(Mukura = Contribution),
  by = "Variable"
)

print(compare_vars)

```

```{r}
# Define path
output_path <- "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/compare_vars.csv"

# Save as CSV
write.csv(compare_vars, file = output_path, row.names = FALSE)

cat("Table exported as CSV to:", output_path, "\n")

```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

compare_vars_long <- compare_vars %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c("Gishwati","Mukura"), names_to = "Area", values_to = "Contribution")

ggplot(compare_vars_long, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Contribution), y = Contribution, fill = Area)) +
  geom_bar(stat="identity", position="dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title="Variable Importance Comparison: Gishwati vs Mukura",
       x="Variable", y="% Contribution")

```

Updates Code

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)
library(dplyr)

# Convert to long format
compare_vars_long <- compare_vars %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c("Gishwati", "Mukura"),
               names_to = "Area",
               values_to = "Contribution")

# Create the plot with custom colors
ggplot(compare_vars_long, aes(x = reorder(Variable, Contribution), y = Contribution, fill = Area)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = "dodge") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Variable Importance Comparison: Gishwati vs Mukura",
    x = "Variable",
    y = "% Contribution"
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Gishwati" = "#298c8c", "Mukura" = "#f1a226"),
    name = "Area"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
    axis.text.y = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(face = "bold")
  )

```

Export

```{r}
  )

# Close the PDF device
dev.off()
```

END

\
\
VARIABLE SELECTION (CORRELATION + JACKKNIFE)

```{r}
# Load libraries
library(terra)
library(usdm)      # for VIF and correlation
library(dplyr)
library(dismo)     # for MaxEnt utilities

# ---- Function to compute correlation ----
compute_correlation <- function(raster_stack, sample_size = 10000, cor_threshold = 0.8) {
  # Step 1: Sample raster values
  sampled_vals <- terra::as.data.frame(
    terra::spatSample(raster_stack, size = sample_size, na.rm = TRUE, method = "random")
  )
  
  # Step 2: Keep only numeric columns
  numeric_cols <- sampled_vals[sapply(sampled_vals, is.numeric)]
  
  # Step 3: Compute correlation matrix
  cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_cols, use = "complete.obs", method = "pearson")
  
  # Step 4: Identify highly correlated pairs (above threshold)
  high_cor_pairs <- which(abs(cor_matrix) > cor_threshold & abs(cor_matrix) < 1, arr.ind = TRUE)
  
  # Step 5: Format results into a dataframe
  if (length(high_cor_pairs) == 0) {
    message("No pairs found with correlation > ", cor_threshold)
    return(data.frame())
  }
  
  cor_pairs <- unique(t(apply(high_cor_pairs, 1, sort)))
  colnames(cor_pairs) <- c("Var1", "Var2")
  
  correlated_df <- as.data.frame(cor_pairs)
  correlated_df$correlation <- mapply(
    function(i, j) cor_matrix[i, j], 
    correlated_df$Var1, 
    correlated_df$Var2
  )
  
  return(correlated_df)
}



```

```{r}

# ---- Compute correlation for Gishwati ----
message("=== Correlation for Gishwati ===")
cor_gishwati <- compute_correlation(terra::rast(gishwati_stack_r), sample_size = 10000, cor_threshold = 0.8)

# ---- Compute correlation for Mukura ----
message("=== Correlation for Mukura ===")
cor_mukura <- compute_correlation(terra::rast(mukura_stack_r), sample_size = 10000, cor_threshold = 0.8)


```

```{r}
# ---- Optional: Export results ----
write.csv(cor_gishwati,
          "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/correlation_gishwati.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

write.csv(cor_mukura,
          "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/correlation_mukura.csv",
          row.names = FALSE)

```

```{r}
# ---- Load libraries ----
library(terra)
library(usdm)      # for VIF and correlation
library(dplyr)
library(dismo)     # for MaxEnt utilities
library(corrplot)  # for correlation visualization

# ---- Function to compute correlation matrix ----
compute_correlation_matrix <- function(raster_stack, sample_size = 10000) {
  # Ensure input is SpatRaster
  raster_stack <- terra::rast(raster_stack)
  
  # Sample raster values
  sampled_vals <- terra::as.data.frame(
    terra::spatSample(raster_stack, size = sample_size, na.rm = TRUE, method = "random")
  )
  
  # Keep only numeric columns
  numeric_cols <- sampled_vals[sapply(sampled_vals, is.numeric)]
  
  # Compute Pearson correlation
  cor_matrix <- cor(numeric_cols, use = "complete.obs", method = "pearson")
  
  return(cor_matrix)
}

# ---- Function to plot and save correlation heatmap ----
plot_correlation <- function(cor_matrix, title, file_path) {
  pdf(file = file_path, width = 8, height = 8)  # Save as PDF
  corrplot(
    cor_matrix,
    method = "color",
    type = "upper",
    tl.col = "black",
    tl.srt = 45,
    
    number.cex = 0.7,
    title = title,
    mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0)
  )
  dev.off()
}

# ---- Compute and plot for Gishwati ----
message("=== Correlation for Gishwati ===")
cor_gishwati_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(gishwati_stack_r)

plot_correlation(
  cor_gishwati_matrix,
  title = "Correlation Matrix - Gishwati",
  file_path = "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/correlation_gishwati.pdf"
)

# ---- Compute and plot for Mukura ----
message("=== Correlation for Mukura ===")
cor_mukura_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(mukura_stack_r)

plot_correlation(
  cor_mukura_matrix,
  title = "Correlation Matrix - Mukura",
  file_path = "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/correlation_mukura.pdf"
)

```

```{r}
# Load libraries
library(terra)
library(usdm)
library(dplyr)
library(dismo)
library(corrplot)

# ---- Function to compute correlation matrix ----
compute_correlation_matrix <- function(raster_stack, sample_size = 10000) {
  sampled_vals <- terra::as.data.frame(
    terra::spatSample(raster_stack, size = sample_size, na.rm = TRUE, method = "random")
  )
  numeric_cols <- sampled_vals[sapply(sampled_vals, is.numeric)]
  cor(numeric_cols, use = "complete.obs", method = "pearson")
}

# ---- Function to extract high correlation pairs ----
extract_high_cor_pairs <- function(cor_matrix, threshold = 0.8) {
  high_cor_pairs <- which(abs(cor_matrix) > threshold & abs(cor_matrix) < 1, arr.ind = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    Var1 = rownames(cor_matrix)[high_cor_pairs[, 1]],
    Var2 = colnames(cor_matrix)[high_cor_pairs[, 2]],
    Correlation = round(cor_matrix[high_cor_pairs], 3)
  )
}

# ---- Function to plot filtered heatmap ----
plot_filtered_corr <- function(cor_matrix, threshold, title) {
  cor_masked <- cor_matrix
  cor_masked[abs(cor_masked) < threshold] <- NA
  corrplot(
    cor_masked,
    method = "color",
    type = "upper",
    tl.col = "black",
    tl.srt = 45,
    addCoef.col = "black",
    number.cex = 0.7,
    title = title,
    mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0)
  )
}


```

```{r}
# ---- Compute and plot for Gishwati ----
message("=== Correlation for Gishwati ===")
cor_gishwati_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(terra::rast(gishwati_stack_r))
cor_gishwati_pairs <- extract_high_cor_pairs(cor_gishwati_matrix, threshold = 0.8)
print("Highly correlated pairs for Gishwati:")
print(cor_gishwati_pairs)

plot_filtered_corr(cor_gishwati_matrix, threshold = 0.8, title = "Gishwati: Correlation > 0.8")

# ---- Compute and plot for Mukura ----
message("=== Correlation for Mukura ===")
cor_mukura_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(terra::rast(mukura_stack_r))
cor_mukura_pairs <- extract_high_cor_pairs(cor_mukura_matrix, threshold = 0.8)
print("Highly correlated pairs for Mukura:")
print(cor_mukura_pairs)

plot_filtered_corr(cor_mukura_matrix, threshold = 0.8, title = "Mukura: Correlation > 0.8")


```

```{r}
# ---- Gishwati ----
pdf("Gishwati_correlation.pdf", width = 10, height = 10)
plot_filtered_corr(cor_gishwati_matrix, threshold = 0.8, title = "Gishwati: Correlation > 0.8")
dev.off()

# ---- Mukura ----
pdf("Mukura_correlation.pdf", width = 10, height = 10)
plot_filtered_corr(cor_mukura_matrix, threshold = 0.8, title = "Mukura: Correlation > 0.8")
dev.off()

```

Predictor Variable Selection

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(terra)
library(corrplot)

### ---- Helper Functions ----

# Select variables based on contribution and correlation
select_variables <- function(contrib_df, cor_matrix, area_name, top_n = 18, cor_threshold = 0.8) {
  
  # 1. Sort variables by contribution
  contrib_ranked <- contrib_df %>%
    arrange(desc(Contribution))
  
  # 2. Keep top N variables
  top_vars <- contrib_ranked$Variable[1:top_n]
  
  # 3. Check correlation within the top variables
  cor_top <- cor_matrix[top_vars, top_vars, drop = FALSE]
  
  selected_vars <- c()
  removed_vars <- c()
  
  for (var in top_vars) {
    if (!(var %in% removed_vars)) {
      selected_vars <- c(selected_vars, var)
      correlated <- names(which(abs(cor_top[var, ]) > cor_threshold & names(cor_top[var, ]) != var))
      if (length(correlated) > 0) {
        removed_vars <- c(removed_vars, correlated)
      }
    }
  }
  
  # Remaining low contribution vars (not in top_n)
  low_contrib_vars <- setdiff(contrib_df$Variable, top_vars)
  removed_vars <- unique(c(removed_vars, low_contrib_vars))
  
  tibble(
    Area = area_name,
    Status = c(rep("Selected for Model", length(selected_vars)),
               rep("Removed", length(removed_vars))),
    Variable = c(selected_vars, removed_vars)
  )
}

```

```{r}
# ---- 1. Prepare contribution data ----
gishwati_contrib <- compare_vars %>%
  select(Variable, Contribution = Gishwati) %>%
  arrange(desc(Contribution))

mukura_contrib <- compare_vars %>%
  select(Variable, Contribution = Mukura) %>%
  arrange(desc(Contribution))

# ---- 2. Compute correlation matrices ----
cor_gishwati_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(terra::rast(gishwati_stack_r))
cor_mukura_matrix <- compute_correlation_matrix(terra::rast(mukura_stack_r))


```

```{r}
# Inspect variable names in the contribution table
print(gishwati_contrib$Variable)

# Inspect variable names in the correlation matrix
print(colnames(cor_gishwati_matrix))

```

```{r}
# Remove ".contribution" suffix from contribution table variable names
gishwati_contrib$Variable <- gsub("\\.contribution$", "", gishwati_contrib$Variable)
mukura_contrib$Variable   <- gsub("\\.contribution$", "", mukura_contrib$Variable)

```

```{r}
# ---- 3. Select variables for both areas ----
selected_table_gishwati <- select_variables(gishwati_contrib, cor_gishwati_matrix, "Gishwati", top_n = 18, cor_threshold = 0.8)
selected_table_mukura   <- select_variables(mukura_contrib, cor_mukura_matrix, "Mukura", top_n = 18, cor_threshold = 0.8)

# ---- 4. Combine into one summary table ----
final_selection_table <- bind_rows(selected_table_gishwati, selected_table_mukura)

# ---- 5. Print results ----
cat("\n=== Final Variable Selection Table ===\n")
print(final_selection_table)



```

```{r}
# ---- 6. Export table ----
output_path <- "/Users/blaise/Rwanda_National Parks/Code_Species_Modeling/Figures_publication/selected_variables_table.csv"
write.csv(final_selection_table, output_path, row.names = FALSE)
cat("\nTable saved at:", output_path, "\n")


```

Preparations of points

Create a Combined Presence Column

Extract Presence and Background Points with Coordinates

```{r}
library(dplyr)

# Call select and mutate explicitly from dplyr
fia_csv1 <- dplyr::mutate(
  fia_csv1,
  any_species_presence = ifelse(
    Acacia_mearnsii_presence == 1 | 
    Acacia_melanoxylon_presence == 1 | 
    Alnus_acuminata_presence == 1, 
    1, 0
  )
)

maxent_data <- dplyr::select(
  fia_csv1,
  Central_coordinate_Longitude,
  Central_coordinate_Latitude,
  any_species_presence
) %>% na.omit()

```


```{r}
# Presence and absence data
presence_points <- maxent_data[maxent_data$any_species_presence == 1, c("Central_coordinate_Longitude", "Central_coordinate_Latitude")]
background_points <- maxent_data[maxent_data$any_species_presence == 0, c("Central_coordinate_Longitude", "Central_coordinate_Latitude")]


```

```{r}
                                               geom = c("x", "y"), 
                                               crs = "EPSG:4326")
```

MODELLING

```{r}
############################################################
# Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) Chunks with Annotations
# Sites analyzed separately: Gishwati and Mukura
# Algorithms: GAM (mgcv), RF (randomForest), MAXENT (maxnet)
# Cross-validation: LOOCV if n_pres < 30, otherwise k-fold (k=3 by default)
# Returns: model metrics (AUC, AUC-PR, TSS), ensemble predictions, 
#          variable-importance plots, and suitability maps (current only)
#
# IMPORTANT:
#   - Functions keep generic arguments (site_name, species_name, presence_xy, background_xy, predictors_stack).
#   - YOU supply the actual objects when calling wrappers (see examples at bottom).
#
# Color consistency for site polygons:
#   Gishwati = #298c8c   |   Mukura = #f1a226
############################################################

# ---- 0) Packages -----------------------------------------------------------
# Install/load packages needed

# ---- 1) Site split & unions ------------------------------------------------
# Input: gmb_reserve_vect (SpatVector of the reserve polygons)
# Actual object needed: gmb_reserve_vect (already in your workspace)

# ---- 2) Any-species presence (OPTIONAL) -----------------------------------
# Input: fia_csv1 (if you want a combined presence variable)
# Otherwise, skip — you already have species-specific objects.

# ---- 3) Selected predictors per site --------------------------------------
# Actual objects needed:
#   gishwati_stack_r, mukura_stack_r   (full predictor stacks)
#   selected_gishwati, selected_mukura (vector of selected variable names)

# The script selects only your chosen predictors:
#   gishwati_stack_selected <- gishwati_stack_r[[selected_gishwati]]
#   mukura_stack_selected   <- mukura_stack_r[[selected_mukura]]

# ---- 4) Background points --------------------------------------------------
# Actual objects needed:
#   background_gishwati_xy_clean (your provided dataframe with columns x,y)
#   background_mukura_xy_clean   (same for Mukura)
#
# NOTE: If these already exist, they will be used. No new sampling.

# ---- 5) Utilities: DO NOT REPLACE -----------------------------------------
# Functions defined here are helpers. Leave as is.
#   - extract_env(xy, stack):
#         xy    = dataframe with x,y coordinates (species presence or background)
#         stack = SpatRaster predictor stack for that site (e.g. gishwati_stack_selected)
#   - make_folds(y): creates folds for CV. Input y = presence/absence vector.
#   - calc_metrics(...): computes AUC, AUC-PR, TSS, etc.
#   - vi_*(): variable importance helpers. Leave as is.

# ---- 6) Core function ------------------------------------------------------
# run_sdm_species_site(...)
# Arguments (you SUPPLY actual objects when calling):
#   site_name      = "Gishwati" or "Mukura" (character string)
#   species_name   = e.g. "Acacia_mearnsii" (character string)
#   presence_xy    = dataframe of species presences for that site, with columns x,y
#                    e.g. acacia_mearnsii_points_gishwati
#   background_xy  = site-specific background dataframe, with columns x,y
#                    e.g. background_gishwati_xy_clean
#   predictors_stack = site-specific predictor raster stack
#                      e.g. gishwati_stack_selected
#
# Inside this function, DO NOT replace anything. You just pass the correct objects.

# ---- 7) Wrappers -----------------------------------------------------------
# run_site_bundle(...)
# This runs all species for a given site.
# Arguments you must provide:
#   site_name       = "Gishwati" or "Mukura"
#   predictors_stack = gishwati_stack_selected or mukura_stack_selected
#   pres_list_xy    = named list of species presence dataframes for that site
#                     e.g. list("Acacia_mearnsii" = acacia_mearnsii_points_gishwati, ...)
#   background_xy   = background_gishwati_xy_clean or background_mukura_xy_clean

# ---- 8) Example calls ------------------------------------------------------
# REPLACE with your actual objects. Examples:

# Gishwati species presence objects (already clipped to site)
pres_gishwati <- list(
  "Acacia_mearnsii"    = acacia_mearnsii_points_gishwati,     # dataframe with x,y
  "Acacia_melanoxylon" = acacia_melanoxylon_points_gishwati,  # dataframe with x,y
  "Alnus_acuminata"    = alnus_acuminata_points_gishwati      # dataframe with x,y
)
results_gishwati <- run_site_bundle(
  site_name = "Gishwati",
  predictors_stack = gishwati_stack_selected,
  pres_list_xy = pres_gishwati,
  background_xy = background_gishwati_xy_clean
)

# Mukura species presence objects
pres_mukura <- list(
  "Acacia_mearnsii"    = acacia_mearnsii_points_mukura,
  "Acacia_melanoxylon" = acacia_melanoxylon_points_mukura,
  "Alnus_acuminata"    = alnus_acuminata_points_mukura
)
results_mukura <- run_site_bundle(
  site_name = "Mukura",
  predictors_stack = mukura_stack_selected,
  pres_list_xy = pres_mukura,
  background_xy = background_mukura_xy_clean
)

# ---- 9) Site-level ensemble (optional) ------------------------------------
# Leave function as is. Supply results_gishwati or results_mukura.
# Example:
#   mean_gishwati <- site_mean_ensemble(results_gishwati)
#   hotspot_gishwati <- make_hotspot(mean_gishwati, thr = 0.6)

# ---- 10) Risk evaluation ---------------------------------------------------
# Leave function as is. Supply:
#   ensemble_raster = results_gishwati[["Acacia_mearnsii"]]$rasters$ENSEMBLE
#   presence_xy     = acacia_mearnsii_points_gishwati
#   forest_mask     = your forest mask SpatRaster

# ---- 11) Plot helpers ------------------------------------------------------
# Already coded. Supply your site polygons if needed.

############################################################
# END with annotations
############################################################

```

```{r}
############################################################
# Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) Chunks
# Sites analyzed separately: Gishwati and Mukura
# Algorithms: GAM (mgcv), RF (randomForest), MAXENT (maxnet)
# Cross‑validation: LOOCV if n_pres < 30, otherwise k-fold (k=3 by default)
# Returns: model metrics (AUC, AUC-PR, TSS), ensemble predictions, 
#          variable-importance plots, and suitability maps (current only)
#
# KEY DESIGN DECISION (important):
# We model **by site first, then by species** within each site (species × site).
# Rationale: environmental gradients, background availability, and sampling
# intensity differ strongly between sites; using site-specific background
# avoids leakage and makes model transfer/metrics fair.
#
# Presence = species-specific points; Background = site-specific (not species).
#
# Color consistency for site polygons (when overlaid on maps):
#   Gishwati = #298c8c   |   Mukura = #f1a226
############################################################


```

```{r}
# ---- 0) Packages -----------------------------------------------------------
required_pkgs <- c(
  "terra", "sf", "dplyr", "tidyr", "pROC", "PRROC", "mgcv",
  "randomForest", "maxnet", "ggplot2", "tmap", "broom"
)
for (p in required_pkgs) if (!requireNamespace(p, quietly = TRUE)) install.packages(p)

library(terra)
library(sf)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(pROC)
library(PRROC)
library(mgcv)
library(randomForest)
library(maxnet)
library(ggplot2)
library(tmap)
library(broom)


```

```{r}



# ---- 2) Any-species presence (if desired for quick checks) -----------------
# Example shows how any-species presence was created from FIA CSV
# (Assumes 'fia_csv1' is in memory and has *_presence binary columns)
# Feel free to skip if already done.
if (exists("fia_csv1")) {
  fia_csv1 <- dplyr::mutate(
    fia_csv1,
    any_species_presence = ifelse(
      Acacia_mearnsii_presence == 1 |
      Acacia_melanoxylon_presence == 1 |
      Alnus_acuminata_presence   == 1, 1, 0
    )
  )

  maxent_data <- dplyr::select(
    fia_csv1,
    Central_coordinate_Longitude,
    Central_coordinate_Latitude,
    any_species_presence
  ) %>% tidyr::drop_na()
}

# ---- 3) Selected predictors per site (inputs must exist) -------------------
# Expect: gishwati_stack_r, mukura_stack_r, selected_gishwati, selected_mukura
stopifnot(exists("gishwati_stack_r"), exists("mukura_stack_r"))
stopifnot(exists("selected_gishwati"), exists("selected_mukura"))

gishwati_stack_selected <- gishwati_stack_r[[selected_gishwati]]
mukura_stack_selected   <- mukura_stack_r[[selected_mukura]]

# ---- 4) Helper: ensure background points per site --------------------------
# Background must be site-specific, not species-specific. If you already have
# background_*_xy_clean as data.frames with columns x,y, they will be used.
# Otherwise we sample pseudo-absence/background within the site polygon.


# Use provided background or create new
# ---- Site Polygons ----
gmb_reserve_sf <- sf::st_as_sf(gmb_reserve_vect)
gishwati_sf <- subset(gmb_reserve_sf, grepl("Gishwati", source))
mukura_sf   <- subset(gmb_reserve_sf, grepl("Mukura", source))
gishwati_vect <- vect(st_union(gishwati_sf))
mukura_vect   <- vect(st_union(mukura_sf))

# ---- Selected Predictors ----
gishwati_stack_selected <- gishwati_stack_r[[selected_gishwati]]
mukura_stack_selected   <- mukura_stack_r[[selected_mukura]]


```

